speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Tape three, Jerry Shane. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: One incident happened to me, amongst many incidents-- anti-Semitic incidents. I was tending cows in Garwolin, in a very nice little town. By the way, most of these towns are the railroad line heading toward Treblinka and Majdanek. Going from the direction of Warsaw, passing my home town Otwock, Garwolin, then comes Demblin, Lublin-- where Majdanek was situated, still is-- and Treblinka nearby. And unknown to me then. I'm tending cows in a public pasture with a bunch of other boys. They went swimming in the nude. The luxury was not permitted for me being circumcised. I tending their cows. They figure, why I'm such a nice guy not going swimming in the nude with them. They want to find out if I'm male or female, or maybe I'm Jewish, or gypsy. And they held me down. And as they realized I'm circumcised, while they were holding me down these boys-- these were teenagers, 16,17. I was about 10, 11 years old. They urinated in my mouth as I was screaming. And they put cow manure, fresh cow manure in my mouth. I managed to free myself and I escaped them. Another incident happened, also very similar. A little town on the same railroad station, as I was going around begging was being liquidated. From a distance, I had seen Jews being loaded onto cattle cars. And a bunch of drunken men passing through the fields, and they see me walking towards the railroad station where the Jews were being loaded on. And they decided to grab me and see if I am one of the Jewish children. And they held me down and see I'm circumcised. They found I was circumcised. While they were so drunk and they were debating what to do with me, I found the first opportunity and I took a run for my life. And I escaped them. And they never caught up with me because I was running for my life. And many other incidents happened similar on the trains I have seen. I was in Garwolin, that wonderful little town. I met some wonderful, nice people, Gentiles. Who were really wonderful to me even after the war. The war didn't end yet but the Russians came in. And there was a family who wanted to take me in as their own. They liked me the way I tended the cow. And they suspected me of being Jewish. And during the war, I came as a beggar going from house to house. And I came to this house. And their name was Krol, it means king. And they-- the man of the house, he was the son. And he told me to go in the bar. He bated me to go in to the barn. And went out to take buy me new clothes, a suit of clothes, with cotton inside and also weather proof. And he told me to hide in the haystack. And if somebody finds me, to not tell them that-- that he is helping me. Say I just snuck into the barn. He came with a suit of clothes and told me never to go back to the trains. Because if I see any Jews jumping off the trains, cattle cars heading for Treblinka and Majdanek, I shouldn't go to their assistance. Just stay away from the trains. Period. Well I didn't do it. I continued going on to the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've seen first as a passenger train, which I used to ride daily. It would pull off to the side track. Everybody on the train knew. A train load full of Jews in cattle cars heading in the direction of Treblinka and Majdanek, now to know where they were heading. Jews would manage still to jump off the fast extreme moving trains. And you seen--

speaker: interviewer
text: How would they jump off the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not know how they managed to jump because each window-- each cattle car had two little windows. And the windows had barbed wire. On top of the rooftop would be manned by an Ukrainian, Estonian, a Lithuanian dressed in German uniform with machine gun. And shoot anybody jumping out off the cattle car trains. I've seen with my own eyes. And then I saw a cattle train pass by my passenger tried and go back onto the track. And following the train who just passed us by, as we were riding the passenger train, I seen packs of men, and women, and children. Polish. Walking alongside the track capturing Jews who jumped off the cattle cars. Who are just managed limping alongside the tracks, laying dead, or is unconscious, or walking in daze. And round them up with one German. They would be accompanied by one German with a machine gun. And as they rounded them up, they made them dig a common grave, and take their clothes off first, so their clothes wouldn't get bloody. Now I understand why they undressed them. And the clothes would be a reward to the Poles who happened to round up these Jews who jumped off those first cattle cars heading to the direction of Treblinka and Majdanek from the direction of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were an eyewitness to this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've seen it myself many a times. And one time in my home railroad station, my hometown railroad station, I became very sloppy and felt sure of myself. I'm sleeping on the windowsill. A big picture window facing the platform of the rail station, Otwock. I woke up to the tune of a whistle and machine gun firing. As I look out the window, I see on the platform there are spotlights shining to a bunch of men, and women, children naked. And as soon as the whistle blew, the machine gun gunned them down and the people would fall on top of each other. In back of me in the station, Polish black marketeers looking out the window like watching a TV movie. And as I proceeded towards my hiding place behind the bookcase to hide underneath the radiator, a Polish boy, a teenager, wearing nice, shiny boots-- which was a status symbol for rich men, took it off some Jewish man, with a sheepskin fur coat, which was also a status symbol-- and he sees me, recognizing me. He was a few years older than me. He was a collaborator with the Germans helping capture Jews. And started calling me in German, first he called me, Yankl. And I pretend that I don't know him. I start running towards my bookcase. And he started calling to the Germans, there's a Jew over here. A Jew over here, to the Germans. And I run behind the bookcase. And I crawled underneath the radiator to lay the tall legs. And people was sitting all around the bookcase, on top, and on the floor around me. And I hold my mouth shut, and I urinated all over myself, and I kept saying the Shema. I didn't come from a religious home, but one thing I knew, somehow instinct to, Shema Adonai Eloheinu Shema Israel. And I kept saying over and over. I heard a woman being interrogated in front of my bookcase, being accused she's Jewish. And the people with her kept saying, no, she's not Jewish. They know her. And I fell asleep from fear alone. I fell asleep thinking, I'm dead. So I didn't want to see myself dying. When I woke up, everything was moving. Trains were going all different directions. I climbed up from behind the bookcase and continued my journey riding the trains back and forth. And another incident happened to other people. In Warsaw, Warsaw had three main railroad-- railroad stations. It was [NON-ENGLISH], it means the main depot. And [NON-ENGLISH], which means the Western. And Praga-- three railroad-- [INAUDIBLE] on the main railroad depot in Warsaw. And the Germans used to capture Poles, and put them on trucks and send them up to forced labor camps to Germany, because they needed manpower. And any Poles that made the wrong move, they'd shoot them right on sight. There were 14, 15-year-olds, it didn't matter. And the same Poles-- I couldn't believe myself-- would capture a Jew and turn him over to the Germans at the same moment. And they would round up, and circle, and surround the station, let's say. And grab the Poles and they would put them on-- on tracks and send them off to Germany. I would figure I would like to offer myself to go to Germany. But one, I was too young. Second, I had to go for a physical checkup, I was told. I'm circumcised. Nobody told me this but I somehow overheard, and you're learning. You become like a busybody. You know everything what's going on around you.

words.text: I had to go for a

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's a man with a bottle of whiskey in his hand dressed like a Jew, Orthodox Jew. Could imagine, an Orthodox Jew on a railroad station in Warsaw, in the middle of the war, drunk as drunk could be, singing songs, and dressed like a Hasidic Jew? And all the Poles over there on the station round him up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the way, there was a Klezmer band, they would call it, Capella. It was playing music to entertain the people over there, while on the train-- on the station, because they had many professional entertainers that used to pass the hat around. So this drunken Jew, supposedly Jew, dancing to the music. And all the Poles were pushing him back and forth like throwing a ball back and forth. And before I realized, whistles start blowing, and Germans surrounded the whole bunch of them, and hauled them all off on trucks to forced labor. They shot some of them who tried to make an escape on the station in Warsaw. Another incident happened, I remember, where I was working in Garwolin-- which was my favorite little town. The station was a little bit away from town. And there were Germans unloading hay for the horses. And there were Jews unloading for them for the German army, the Wehrmacht. And the Jews were eating the same as the German soldiers, soup. It was lunch time. And the field kitchen they had. And I didn't tell the Jews who I am. And they gave me a bowl of soup, and I was eating the soup. A Jewish man gave it to me without telling him that I'm Jewish. And one of the soldiers went to relieve himself in a hay stack. He came back, I think it was a half a dozen, five, or six Jewish teenage boys. Jewish-- later on I'd discover they were Jewish-- and with a woman crying, she's not German. She's not Jewish, she's German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her husband ask her, how do you get hiding in the haystack? She says, I was taking a train somewheres and they make me jump off the train. I jumped off, and I was hiding in the haystack not to be arrested again. Because she-- my husband is Jewish, was Jewish-- and put into the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not Jewish, she says. My children are not Jewish, because I'm not Jewish. The Germans offered the soldiers-- I said soldiers, on-the-line soldiers, the Wehrmacht-- to save her life. To go about where she wants to. But the children they've got to shoot. And she comes out of the woods, shoot me, too, if you're shooting them. They done away with all of them. And at first the women who was flirting with the Germans waited to-- for them to undress themselves, so they would get their clothes as a reward. Another incident happened on this little railroad station in Garwolin. I-- I'm sitting eating a bowl of soup. And as I'm looking out the window facing the railroad track, and I seen a very familiar man who is a Volksdeutsche from the little town. He didn't know me. I just seen him, watched him-- young man, wearing different uniforms at all different times, whatever suited him-- with his gun in his hand. And a bunch of Polish men and woman stampeding this very beautifully dressed woman. One woman in her stockings already-- someone took her shoes away already. And she's pleading for her life in a foreign language unknown to me. It could be French, it could be English, it could be anything. And they took her behind-- he took her with the people behind him. Behind the barn I heard a shot ring out. And they're all laughing, making fun of how she keeled over. And the same Volksdeutsche, German, came into the little station waiting room, which there was nobody there waiting for trains. And he seen me eating the big pot of soup. He grabbed me by the hand, and tried to grab me, accusing me of being a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: The lady, who worked in the kitchen, a really heavyset lady, came out and said to him, he's no Jew. He is my regular beggar over here. He comes regular with the whole family. What's the matter with you? She whispered something in his ear, and they all [INAUDIBLE] kissing and hugging went over across the road to her house. Why do you think she saved you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saved her own self, probably. If she were helping a Jew, she'd be shot like me just the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think of yourself as a Jewish boy during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew there are somebody is out to get me. I wanted to brainwash myself I'm not Jewish. I was a born Jew. After-- before the war even ended, I went to this beautiful little town, Garwolin. I made lots of friends there. They suspected me of being Jewish. There was one family. The man was so nice to me, he was so proud of me, he taught me how to plow. I was little. I couldn't hold down a plow. I was too little. And he'd make me cut the hay with a kind of wheel because I couldn't reach it and he helped me. Called me son. Was so proud of me that I was bathing myself outside in the cold weather. And I had a little pot-- pot over there, washing myself around. And he indicated that he would like to have me as his son. He had two daughters, grown so daughters, who were black marketeers are selling food to the ghetto. And I can hear the daughters saying-- they must have been in their 20s or 30s, the daughters, not married. And there are many black marketeers used to meet over there. And the daughters were saying, they wish they wish they would look more Jewish so they could sneak in closer to the ghetto. And I was so proud to be belonging to them. That one day they brought two boys not known to me. They were Jewish and other black marketeers and they told me to leave. I was very hurt-- like losing a mother, and father, and family. And I continued going about in a different way. And one family, like I told you before, a man who gave me clothes and tried to tell me to not go on the trains after the Russians came in. I went back there and I asked them to take me in now that there were no Germans no more. They told me to go back to my own Jewish people. I was very insulted. I said, I'm not Jewish. They said, don't tell us. If you don't want to, don't go back. But if you are Jewish, go to your own people. It's better than staying with us and living like a peasant. I reluctantly went to Lublin where I met some Jewish-- I thought they were Jewish because they spoke a foreign language to me. They directed me to an orphanage in Lublin. And these were children who survived in the forest. Jewish children in their orphanage after the Russians came in. And they asked me the word [YIDDISH] I didn't speak Yiddish-- now that I know what [YIDDISH] meant until recently. And they wanted to see if I'm circumcised. Now the Gentiles want to see if I'm circumcised, these guys want to see if I'm circumcised, and I had a fight with them. And I walked out in disgust after beating up some boys over there. And I walk out in disgust. And a little boy named Chaim Boorstein, he followed me. He looked very Jewish. And him and I were going to hitchhike towards the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after the war now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was during?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war is still going on but the Russians were there already occupying the area. And going back to the German occupation, if you wish me to continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you need to tell us about the years that you spent riding the trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had many, many collaborators. If not the collaborators, many, many Jews could have survived. A Jew could not possible-- even if he had fictitious papers-- he couldn't survive. A German would never recognize me because of my Jewish accent. Well I had to learn how to speak Polish very quick with a Gentile accent. Which I still speak like one. And a Pole would recognize it and turn us in. A Jew-- I, my personal opinion is all the extermination camps were built in Poland. A Jew who have he escaped off the trains, there were collaborators picking him off the trains and handing him over. Would have been the only reward to them was for a human life to take the person's shoes or his clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when Auschwitz, or Oswiecim, it means, the holy place-- Auschwitz in Polish. All these extermination camps, now they're called concentration camps, were all situated in Poland. Now I don't mean to talk with hatred towards any individual. I'm talking facts, what I've seen it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one time I am-- I discovered two other boys. Jewish boys from my hometown who I didn't-- we didn't know each other from before the war. One of the boys' brother, who lives in Chicago here, belonged to Dr. [NON-ENGLISH], B'nai Brith-- Ben Markov. His brother and I got to talking. And I realized his brother was one of the two boys who lived with me on the trains. And the story goes as follows my experience with his brother. We used to-- three-- two other boys. Between his brother and another boy, we used to go different ways. Everybody did his own shtick. I and him were baking potatoes in a little fireplace just before a big city named Demblin. Because the Gendarme used to come raid the station. Take all the [INAUDIBLE] put it back, all this stuff, the black marketeers one station closer to Warsaw. And this little station located in the forest like. And when the black marketeers used to wait for the train to arrive, the 12 o'clock train would take me to Otwock to sleep behind the bookcase. Of from the days proceeds of being a beggar, we were baking potatoes in a fireplace. And we're selling to the black marketeers the baked potatoes. He was selling potatoes to one of my customers. We got into a fist fight. I wound up sleeping on the outside of the platform, outside the toilet, on top of the attic. As I'm sleeping on the attic, a Polish young man-- 16, 17-- who came looking for contraband of the black marketeers found me instead. Start summoning the German he was with a machine gun, Gendarme. There's a Jew up here. And when I tried to escape to the other side, the Gendarme called with this machine gun. And he took me. And I start howling, I'm not Jewish. The Polish boy started hollering, kill him. Kill him. He's a Jew. And I said, take me to the rail station into this depot. Ask everybody who knows me there. He wouldn't do it. Finally, I convinced him. He took me supposedly walking towards the waiting room of the little railroad station. The station master, an old Polish station master, came out to greet the oncoming train with a lantern. And he told-- the Polish boy told the German, he said that the station master must shine the lantern on my penis and see if I'm circumcised. Instead I didn't want to let him do it. So the boy ordered the German to shoot me. As a German cocked his gun to shoot me, I grabbed the old station master as a shield. And he kept hitting me with the lantern. And he kept hitting me. And finally he convinced the German to let me inside the depot. As they let me inside the little waiting room-- inside it's maybe 20 by 20. A little waiting room packed with people. And the Polish boy calls out, who knows this little Jew, meaning me. A Polish lady sitting next to my boyfriend for baking potatoes said, there's another little Jew right over here. To my boyfriend Moishe Markov. And I was looking for a way to escape. The two women came in to see-- the scouts-- seeing what's going on. The German Gendarme was guarding the door. One door only. And they tried to hold him, frisk him, or something like this. I snuck between their feet, and I run out. It was raining, and I had wooden soled shoes. I rolled myself between the tracks and the platform. And little by little, rolled myself into the forest on my fours. In the meantime, the Germans were shooting blindly into the forest. A Jew escaped, a Jew escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: I found my-- somebody calls up out of the darkness in the forest, who goes there? So the first thing of me to say, a Pole. Naming myself I'm a Pole. And I told them what happened. I said, my mother was smuggling tobacco and whiskey-- which is the death sentence, automatic death sentence. And the Gendarmes arrested her, and I escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: They believed my story. And they all broke off in different ways, the black marketeer ladies and men. And they went to their favorite peasants.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they bedded us down on straw on the floor. As I woke up in the morning, I seen these black marketeer women looking over down at me. Is he a Jew? Is he a gypsy? He looks more like a Jew. They were betting themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before they had a chance, I run away out of this peasant's house and I went back to the railroad station. I went back the railroad station. There was the same lady who was still sitting at the fireplace with my boyfriend, Moishe. And he's making fun out of me, because the Germans were chasing me, looking for Jews all day. And the German and the Polish boy take away lots of goodies from the black marketeers and gave it to him. And the Polish boy is hollering, you got them, dirty Jew. The same lady who called me, there's a another Jew here. She starts screaming at me. Because of you, no black marketeer could board the trains because Gendarmes are raiding this station every day. I got into a fight again with Moishe. And I went over, near the forest-- over there along the railroad track, a lot farther down. Built myself a bonfire. Took my shoes and my shirt off to delouse my clothes. And I heard some bullets flying all around me. I look up on top on the railroad tracks above me. And there's a man kneeling with a rifle aiming at me-- playing like games with me, bullets flying all around me. I left my shoes and my clothes behind me. Now it was learned that when somebody shoots at you, run zigzag. Don't run straight. And then I was completely naked. I kept coming back at night, trying to board a train. And every night, there's black marketeers scattering throughout the fields. And nobody could board the trains. They used to come. And then I've decided to walk closer towards Warsaw and never came back to that railroad station again. And I never seen my boyfriend, Moishe, either. And the other boy, I don't know what happened to him either.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were faced with so much danger. When you were alone, did you ever cry about the situation that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cry now more than I did then. Just reminding myself what happened. Because then I was hardened. I knew this is part of my life and I have to do it. And that's part of my life. And I did it. And I did it connivingly. It was like a game of how many customs I go with-- which village I'm going to go back that's for handouts, my favorite villages. And then I-- like one time, I used to go around-- I love-- I had my favorite little railroad station, only a few miles away from my hometown. There are bunch of butchers had they-- on their houses they were making sausages for the black marketeers. And every railroad station had like a counter that was selling to the black marketeers food, cooked food. And they had lots of sort-- this cafeteria had lots of sausage-- it was a very prosperous looking railroad station. And the man was very generous, giving me pieces of sausage. As I mean the pieces of sausage. And a little man of small stature, a German I'm talking about, a conductor, he grabbed me and tried to call me a Jew. He was so drunk he could walk even straight-- he was almost as tall as I was, and I wasn't tall. I was a little boy. And as he dragged me out the door, he got entangled-- some people coming into the door. And he got entangled with them and I broke away from him. And he was shooting at me blindly in the dark. And I ran back onto the platform on top. And I was hiding in the washroom until the train came a few minutes later and I proceeded again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever want to just give up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever want to give up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. It was a game. It was a game. I even was seen from the outside as my train used to pass by Warsaw. The stench, the smell of the burning flesh I learned later on. It was so terrible. Coming from the from the village, from the farms, and as you come into the city, the stench of the burning ghetto in Warsaw. And by the way, a year later, it was the Gentile uprising, the Polish Gentile uprising. I understand 200-- some odd 1,000 Poles got killed in the Polish uprising. Mind you, the forces what they were united with the Jewish underground, I learned. Backed the Polish resistance to join forces with the Jewish resistance when they had the Jewish uprising in Warsaw, the ghetto uprising. It was the Polish uprising that took place one year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got myself caught in the Gentile Polish uprising. How did it happen? I went to get some candy charms. I got off the railroad station, the main station in Warsaw. And I bought my supply of candy charms. As I'm trying to get back onto the train-- this time Warsaw was very strict. It wasn't easy to sneak into the platform without getting at least a ticket to the nearest station.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I walked over to one window, two windows, three windows-- all manned by men. Usually women would be at the ticket one. They didn't answer me asking for a ticket to the nearest station. All of a sudden, I heard shooting going on. I heard shooting going. I look outside the station. And there are men wearing white and red armbands, and shooting at Germans, Germans shooting at them. And I run out of the station. And I seen a street car loaded full of people. And the only room was on the step. Everybody is hanging on the one step into the little streetcar. And I hang on to body handle and the streetcar left Warsaw, outside Warsaw. And as it came to the outside outskirts of Warsaw, I seen a bunch of Polish people with their hands risen up in the air. And held at gunpoint by Germans. And the streetcar was stopped, and everybody [GERMAN]-- it means, everybody out. And they told everybody to disembark. As everybody was disembarking from the streetcar, I was squeezing myself deeper into the street car. I sat myself down next to the driver. And like really close, tight to the driver in the street car. And the German came in with a machine gun, cocked the machine gun right ready to shoot. And he looked and says, [GERMAN]-- go, keep going. And he thought I belonged to the street car driver. I found myself on the outskirts of Warsaw. And I promptly went to a nearby town where I had a bunch of so-called acquaintances-- Polish boys who were stealing coal from the trains with me. We used to hop the trains and throw coals off the train heading for Germany. Which one of my other vocations was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea what year this might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't care. I still don't care. I don't even know what-- if you can tell me now it's the 22nd of October, I wouldn't even know it. Because I'm leaving for Israel. That's how I know it's the 22nd of October. I wouldn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea, totally, how many years you spent riding these trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I know. Approximately. They look on the calendar and know how much. That I didn't know. Day and night, day and night, day and night, day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five to six years. 1939 to 1945. I would still continue doing it until these people send me to Lublin. And then I was hitchhiking towards the United States with my friend Chaim Boorstein. An adventure we went through, a comic, on our a way to the United States. We didn't get the United States in one of many other orphanages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how your experience on the train finally came to an end. Where you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living with these Polish people. These boys who are stealing things, would be selling candies on the train, tending cows. There were a bunch of boys I had some kind of connection. One connection to the other one. And the Polish army came in. And everybody was joyful, they were attacking the tanks, kissing the tanks. And then everybody was so happy and joyful. And I was sleeping. And the people I was staying with, there was no fathers. And the mother, and the sons, and there was a daughter living there-- my age, all these children. And they pulled the sheet down and found me to be circumcised. And they gave me hell for risking their lives. Which they are right. They would have captured them, killed them, and kill me, too-- together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of the boys named Wadik-- Walter would be in English. Him and I used to go to Warsaw. They recruited Polish people to dig anti-tank ditches for the Germans. And we used to go to Warsaw. And Warsaw was liquidated.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nobody living in Warsaw by the way-- after the Polish uprising. Warsaw was leveled to the ground by the Germans with a cannon named Bertha. It was on railroad tracks. And I used to stand about 20 some odd feet away from the cannon and seen the Germans firing onto Warsaw. And they took all the people out of Warsaw. Taking them away in passenger trains, not like-- unlike the Jews. Even the train stopped periodically on a little station. I sold all my candy charms to the people on the trains, Gentile people who had been taken away to some place unknown destination.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were still doing that after you were freed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. That's before I was freed.

speaker: interviewer
text: No? This was before you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I was freed. After I was freed, while going into Warsaw-- before I was freed, that is-- we used to go as a reward to us who used to go into Warsaw proper and take whatever we could find. Because no people lived there. I took an accordion, a bicycle, a Polish man's uniform. And they forced me to sell the accordion to them at whatever money they wanted to give me as threatening me. Said, you risk your life, you better sell that accordion for a price to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had money, in other words. When I came to the orphanage in Lublin. So that's as a result with these funds, I had the money, and Chaim Boorstein knew the geography. So we started hitchhiking towards the United States. That was an adventure by itself.
